meta.name: DREFS Unified Package
meta.author: Oleksii Kartashov
meta.sync_note: 237 entries from bcio_index + 23 entries from BCIO_CANDIDATE_RANKER not present in index. For the 23 additions: hierarchy taken from primary BCIO module (BCT or MoA); drefs_assignment=ranker_semantic.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention outcome behaviour
entries.definition: Human behaviour that is an intervention outcome.

entries.label: social alienation
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to perceive or experience oneself as isolated from and not meaningfully involved in social groups.

entries.label: awareness
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> that is realized by experiencing events, objects, sensory patterns or cognitive representations.

entries.label: belief about barriers to a behaviour
entries.definition: A <belief> about a factor that could restrict or impede a person from engaging in a behaviour.
children: belief about effort required for a behaviour

entries.label: belief about gain
entries.definition: A <belief> about the existence of an increase from an initial reference point.

entries.label: belief about reduction
entries.definition: A <belief> about the existence of a decrease from an initial reference point.

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour and its associated outcomes
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> to organise and execute a behaviour and achieve the outcomes associated with this behaviour.

entries.label: situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> when a barrier or facilitator of the behaviour is encountered.
children: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour under conditions of stress
self-efficacy belief for a behaviour in the face of social pressure

entries.label: situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> when a barrier or facilitator of the behaviour is encountered.
children: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour under conditions of stress
self-efficacy belief for a behaviour in the face of social pressure

entries.label: motivation to comply
entries.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act in accordance with the likely approval of others.

entries.label: motivational orientation
entries.definition: A <motivational disposition> to be guided by a type of outcome.

entries.label: prevention focused motivational orientation
entries.definition: A <motivational orientation> in which motivation is guided by a focus on the presence or absence of negative outcomes.

entries.label: promotion focused motivational orientation
entries.definition: A <motivational orientation> in which motivation is guided by a focus on the presence or absence of positive outcomes.

entries.label: need for autonomy
entries.definition: A <psychological need> to have a sense of personal agency and initiative.

entries.label: healthcare access
entries.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> regarding how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

entries.label: physical behavioural opportunity
entries.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves time and parts of the environmental system that do not involve people or organisations.

entries.label: social behavioural opportunity
entries.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves the social environmental system.

entries.label: plan enactment
entries.definition: A <bodily process> by which a person attempts to follow the steps in a plan.

entries.label: feeling an urge
entries.definition: A <subjective affective feeling> that  arises when an impulse to enact a behaviour is activated but not immediately acted upon.

entries.label: comprehension
entries.definition: A <cognitive process> that creates a representation of the meaning and significance of a communication, object, event or situation.

entries.label: decision simplification
entries.definition: A <cognitive process> used prior to decision-making that reduces the perceived scope or complexity of available options.

entries.label: decision-making
entries.definition: <Judging> in which one or more propositions or behaviours are identified as preferred from a larger number.
children: decisional balance process about changing behaviour

entries.label: decision-making
entries.definition: <Judging> in which one or more propositions or behaviours are identified as preferred from a larger number.
children: decisional balance process about changing behaviour

entries.label: associative learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that involves changing the strength of association between two or more mental processes.

entries.label: associative learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that involves changing the strength of association between two or more mental processes.

entries.label: associative learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that involves changing the strength of association between two or more mental processes.

entries.label: classical conditioning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> that occurs when the pairing of a conditioned and unconditioned stimulus leads to the conditioned stimulus producing a response that would otherwise have only been produced by the unconditioned stimulus.

entries.label: discrimination learning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which people learn to distinguish between appropriate responses to similar but different stimuli depending on whether a generalised response is rewarded or not.

entries.label: extinction in classical conditioning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which the strength of a conditioned response is reduced because its conditioned stimulus is presented without being accompanied by the unconditioned stimulus.

entries.label: extinction in operant learning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which the rate of behavioural response in the presence of a discriminative stimulus is reduced because the response is no longer followed by a reinforcer.

entries.label: stimulus generalisation in learning
entries.definition: <Generalisation in learning> concerning the antecedent stimulus.

entries.label: operant conditioning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which the likelihood of a particular behavioural response is influenced by the past consequences associated with performing that behaviour.

entries.label: operant conditioning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which the likelihood of a particular behavioural response is influenced by the past consequences associated with performing that behaviour.

entries.label: operant conditioning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which the likelihood of a particular behavioural response is influenced by the past consequences associated with performing that behaviour.

entries.label: associative memory
entries.definition: A <memory process> that deals with the receipt, storage, retrieval and modification of information regarding a set of entities associated with a particular object or event.

entries.label: episodic memory
entries.definition: A <memory process> that deals with the receipt, storage, retrieval and modification of information regarding a personally experienced event at a particular time or place.

entries.label: episodic memory
entries.definition: A <memory process> that deals with the receipt, storage, retrieval and modification of information regarding a personally experienced event at a particular time or place.

entries.label: semantic memory
entries.definition: A <memory process> that deals with the receipt, storage, retrieval and modification of information associated with meanings, understandings or conceptual facts about the world.

entries.label: semantic memory
entries.definition: A <memory process> that deals with the receipt, storage, retrieval and modification of information associated with meanings, understandings or conceptual facts about the world.

entries.label: behavioural motivation
entries.definition: A <mental process> that energises and directs a behaviour.

entries.label: automatic behavioural motivation
entries.definition: <Behavioural motivation> that arises from emotions and impulses that result from associative learning or innate dispositions.

entries.label: intrinsic motivation
entries.definition: <Autonomous motivation> due to a behaviour's inherent enjoyment, satisfaction or benefits to oneself.

entries.label: intrinsic motivation
entries.definition: <Autonomous motivation> due to a behaviour's inherent enjoyment, satisfaction or benefits to oneself.

entries.label: self-monitoring
entries.definition: A <monitoring process> in which one observes one's own behaviour or mental processes.

entries.label: belief about the credibility of a message's source
entries.definition: A <belief about message> in terms of whether a verbal, graphic, written or recorded communication is provided by people or institutions who provide reliable information.

entries.label: belief about the credibility of a message's source
entries.definition: A <belief about message> in terms of whether a verbal, graphic, written or recorded communication is provided by people or institutions who provide reliable information.

entries.label: evaluative belief about others
entries.definition: An <evaluative belief> about the attributes of others.

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> to organise and execute a behaviour.
children: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour during routine activities

entries.label: approval-based motivation
entries.definition: A <need-based motivation> related to gaining or maintaining social approval or a positive self-evaluation.

entries.label: autonomous motivation
entries.definition: <Behavioural motivation> that is internally generated and based on a behaviour being interesting or valuable to an individual.

entries.label: behavioural importance-based motivation
entries.definition: <Autonomous motivation> due to the perceived value or importance of the behaviour to oneself.

entries.label: extrinsic motivation
entries.definition: <Behaviour motivation> that is based on external rewards, punishments or pressure regarding a behaviour.

entries.label: need-based motivation
entries.definition: <Autonomous motivation> due to a behaviour being perceived to satisfy a psychological need.

entries.label: habit learning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> that involves strengthening an association between a stimulus and an impulse through repetition of co-occurrence of the stimulus and the behaviour.

entries.label: goal setting BCT
entries.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that sets goals.

entries.label: action planning BCT
entries.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that involves making a detailed plan for the performance of the behaviour, which must include at least one of context, frequency, duration or intensity.

entries.label: affirm commitment BCT
entries.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that asks the person to affirm or reaffirm statements indicating commitment to change the behaviour.

entries.label: monitoring BCT
entries.definition: A <behaviour change technique> that involves gathering or using information about performance.

entries.label: observe behaviour without feedback BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> that observes current performance of the behaviour with the person’s knowledge but without providing feedback about their behaviour.

entries.label: record behaviour without feedback BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> that records current performance of the behaviour with the person’s knowledge but without providing feedback about their behaviour.

entries.label: observe outcome of behaviour without feedback BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> that observes an outcome of performing the behaviour with the person’s knowledge but without providing feedback about the outcome.

entries.label: record outcome of behaviour without feedback BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> that records an outcome of performing the behaviour with the person's knowledge but without providing feedback about the outcome.

entries.label: self-monitor behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> in which the person uses a method to monitor and record their behaviour.

entries.label: self-monitor outcome of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <monitoring BCT> in which the person uses a method to monitor and record an outcome of their behaviour.

entries.label: demonstrate the behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <guide how to perform behaviour BCT> that provides an observable sample of the performance of the behaviour for the person to aspire to or imitate.

entries.label: draw attention to incompatible beliefs BCT
entries.definition: A <suggest different perspective on behaviour BCT> that draws the person's attention to the discrepancies between current or past behaviour and self-identity.

entries.label: reduce cue frequency BCTs
entries.definition: An <alter external stimulus BCT> in which cues for the behaviour are presented less frequently.

entries.label: context-specific repetition of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to repeat the behaviour in the same context.

entries.label: context-specific repetition of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to repeat the behaviour in the same context.

entries.label: context-specific repetition of alternative behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to repeat an alternative behaviour consistently in a context that previously elicited an unwanted behaviour.

entries.label: context-specific repetition of alternative behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to repeat an alternative behaviour consistently in a context that previously elicited an unwanted behaviour.

entries.label: reduce exposure to cues for the behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <alter external stimulus BCT> that reduces an external stimulus that signals the behaviour.

entries.label: affirm valued self-identity BCT
entries.definition: A <prompt focus on self-identity BCT> that advises engagement in activities that affirm the person's valued attributes.

entries.label: gradually increase exposure to aversive stimulus BCT
entries.definition: An <expose to stimulus BCT> that involves gradually increasing exposure to an aversive stimulus to reduce the likelihood of the behaviour when encountering that stimulus.

entries.label: review behaviour goal plan BCT
entries.definition: A <goal directed BCT> that reviews progress towards a behavioural goal and considers modifying the plan to achieve the goal.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention schedule of delivery
entries.definition: A BCI attribute that involves its temporal organisation.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention schedule of delivery
entries.definition: A BCI attribute that involves its temporal organisation.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention schedule of delivery
entries.definition: A BCI attribute that involves its temporal organisation.

entries.label: relatedness between person source and the target population
entries.definition: A personal role of source that is realised in some relationship to the characteristics of the intervention participants.

entries.label: organisational source role
entries.definition: A BCI source role that is borne by an organisation.

entries.label: gamification mode of delivery
entries.definition: Mode of delivery that involves application of typical elements of game playing  to other areas of activity, typically as an online marketing technique to encourage engagement with a product or service.

entries.label: participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
entries.definition: Participant engagement with intervention, where the intervention focuses on changing behaviour.

entries.label: participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
entries.definition: Participant engagement with intervention, where the intervention focuses on changing behaviour.

entries.label: participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
entries.definition: Participant engagement with intervention, where the intervention focuses on changing behaviour.

entries.label: organisational role
entries.definition: A role which inheres in a person and is realised by performing the activities deemed appropriate for that particular role as specified in the organisation rules.

entries.label: intervention population
entries.definition: A human population who are exposed to an intervention.

entries.label: ethnic group membership
entries.definition: A self-identity as belonging to a social group or groups whose members have a common cultural or ancestral heritage.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention evaluation study risk of bias or error
entries.definition: An information content entity that is about the likelihood of the BCI evaluation finding misrepresenting the outcome behaviour.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention dose
entries.definition: A BCI content attribute that is its amount.

entries.label: behaviour change intervention dose
entries.definition: A BCI content attribute that is its amount.

entries.label: country of intervention
entries.definition: A geographical location of a country where the intervention takes place.

entries.label: residential care or assisted living
entries.definition: A multiple occupancy residence where multiple vulnerable people live

entries.label: behaviour change intervention study sample
entries.definition: A research study sample whose behaviour is studied in a BCI evaluation study.

entries.label: social organisation behaviour
entries.definition: A socially-related behaviour that involves a person contributing to the functioning of a social structure or a person in relation to a social structure.

entries.label: agenda-sharing communication
entries.definition: A communication in which the initiator and one or more activity participants work together to determine a focus for the activity to be undertaken.

entries.label: active participation encouraging communication style
entries.definition: A communication style marked by fostering the involvement of the recipient as an engaged actor in the activity to be undertaken rather than as a passive recipient.

entries.label: autonomy-supportive communication style
entries.definition: A communication style which aims to promote the recipient's capacity to make their own decisions.

entries.label: engaging communication style
entries.definition: A communication style characterised by attempting to attract and maintain a recipient's interest or attention.

entries.label: patient communication style
entries.definition: A communication style conveying willingness to take time to understand or complete a process.

entries.label: semi-structured communication style
entries.definition: A communication style involving the communication content having some constituent pre-planned parts but also being responsive to inputs.

entries.label: structured communication style
entries.definition: A communication style involving the communication content having constituent pre-planned parts.

entries.label: attentional self-regulation capability
entries.definition: A <self-regulation capability> to modulate one’s attention toward internal or external stimuli.

entries.label: belief about healthcare accessibility
entries.definition: A <belief about one's environment> in terms of how easy it is for a person to approach and use a healthcare service.

entries.label: disengagement due to workload
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to be detached from other people due to exhaustion experienced in one's working environment.

entries.label: disengagement due to workload
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to be detached from other people due to exhaustion experienced in one's working environment.

entries.label: temporal orientation to the future
entries.definition: A <temporal orientation> to focus more on future than present outcomes.

entries.label: temporal orientation to the present
entries.definition: A <temporal orientation> to focus more on present than future outcomes.

entries.label: learning
entries.definition: A <mental process> in which a lasting mental or behavioural change occurs as the result of experience.

entries.label: self-regulation process
entries.definition: A <bodily process> that modulates the frequency, rate or extent of a response to external or internal stimuli and that is instigated by the person.
children: mental rehearsal to guide and motivate behaviour

entries.label: reflective behavioural motivation
entries.definition: <Behavioural motivation> that involves reflective thinking.

entries.label: memory process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that is the encoding, storing, and retrieval of informational stimuli.

entries.label: memory process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that is the encoding, storing, and retrieval of informational stimuli.

entries.label: belief about quality of life
entries.definition: A <belief> about one's position in life in relation to one's goals, expectations, standards and concerns.

entries.label: arranging a healthcare service appointment
entries.definition: Utilising healthcare that involves scheduling a time to interact with a person or organisation providing healthcare.

entries.label: self-monitoring an aspect of health
entries.definition: A health-related behaviour that uses a method to monitor and record an indicator of one’s health or wellbeing.

entries.label: behaviour starting point
entries.definition: A time point that is the start of an individual human behaviour.

entries.label: personal history of intervention exposure for the same outcome
entries.definition: A personal history part of exposure to an intervention targeting the same outcome as the current intervention.

entries.label: personal history of intervention exposure for the same outcome behaviour
entries.definition: A personal history part  that includes exposure to a prior intervention targeting the same outcome behaviour as the current intervention.

entries.label: personal history of same intervention exposure
entries.definition: A personal history part that includes exposure to the same intervention as the current intervention.

entries.label: personal history part of intervention exposure
entries.definition: A personal history part  that includes exposure to one or more interventions.

entries.label: personal history part of intervention exposure
entries.definition: A personal history part  that includes exposure to one or more interventions.

entries.label: research study sample
entries.definition: A population whose behaviour is studied in a research study.

entries.label: active goal
entries.definition: A <goal> that is being pursued at any one point in time.

entries.label: amount of social reinforcement for a behaviour
entries.definition: A <data item> that is about the frequency and strength of social reinforcement that is experienced by the person.

entries.label: attentional disposition
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> that is realised by focusing one's attention on events, objects, sensory patterns or cognitive representations.
children: disposition to attend to one?s goals

entries.label: behaviour maintenance self-efficacy
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> that continues a behaviour pattern.

entries.label: behavioural barrier
entries.definition: A <disposition> that includes low capability, opportunity and motivation to enact a behaviour.

entries.label: behavioural opportunity through information
entries.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that is realised by the provision of accurate information.

entries.label: behavioural recovery self-efficacy
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> following a lapse in that behaviour

entries.label: belief about a behavioural rule
entries.definition: A <belief> about whether there is a social rule that a behaviour must or must not occur in a given situation.
children: belief that a behaviour is forbidden
belief that a behaviour is compulsory

entries.label: belief about effort required for a behaviour
entries.definition: A <belief about barriers to a behaviour> in terms of level of effort required.

entries.label: belief about the positive health consequences of a behaviour
entries.definition: A <belief about health consequences of behaviour> in which the consequences are evaluated as positive.

entries.label: extrinsic social motivation
entries.definition: An <extrinsic motivation> that derives from external rewards, punishments or pressure regarding a behaviour.

entries.label: generality of self-efficacy
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to be self-efficacious across a wide range of situations and behaviours.

entries.label: high motivational disposition to change behaviour
entries.definition: A <personal attribute> in terms of motivational disposition to change behaviour being high.

entries.label: intention for collective behaviour
entries.definition: A <behavioural intention> to enact a behaviour with one or more others.

entries.label: intermittent schedule of reinforcement
entries.definition: A <schedule of reinforcement> in which the event following the behaviour occurs on some but not all occasions.

entries.label: intermittent schedule of reinforcement
entries.definition: A <schedule of reinforcement> in which the event following the behaviour occurs on some but not all occasions.

entries.label: low motivational disposition to change behaviour
entries.definition: A <personal attribute> in terms of motivational disposition to change behaviour being low.

entries.label: low self-efficacy
entries.definition: An <personal attribute> of self-efficacy belief that is low.

entries.label: observational learning capability
entries.definition: A <mental capability> to learn through observation.

entries.label: primary reinforcer
entries.definition: A <reinforcer> whose effect on behaviour is innately determined.

entries.label: primary reinforcer
entries.definition: A <reinforcer> whose effect on behaviour is innately determined.

entries.label: quality of social relationships
entries.definition: A <social environmental system attribute> that is the extent to which social relationships with other people are positive or negative.

entries.label: schedule of reinforcement
entries.definition: A <uniform process aggregate> whose members are reinforcement processes involving an individual over a period of time.

entries.label: schedule of reinforcement
entries.definition: A <uniform process aggregate> whose members are reinforcement processes involving an individual over a period of time.

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for avoiding a threat
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief> that one can take actions to avoid, reduce, or remove a threat or its effects.

entries.label: strength of attention
entries.definition: A <mental process attribute> that is the extent to which attentional resources are focused on a target.

entries.label: technology availability
entries.definition: A <behavioural opportunity> that involves the extend to which technology is available in the environmental system.

entries.label: temporal orientation
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to appraise imagined future occurrences differently according to how far in the future they are judged to occur.

entries.label: vicarious learning
entries.definition: <Observational learning> that occurs through observing the consequences of the actions of others.

entries.label: vicarious learning capability
entries.definition: An <observational learning capability> through the observing the consequences of the actions of others.

entries.label: willingness to adopt new behaviours
entries.definition: A <willingness to change behaviour> to adopt new behaviours.

entries.label: observational learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that occurs through observing others.

entries.label: adherence to healthcare treatment
entries.definition: Participating in healthcare treatment in accordance with treatment instructions.

entries.label: carer monitoring of child behaviour
entries.definition: A monitoring behaviour in which a carer gathers information about the activities of some child in their care.

entries.label: cue management behaviour
entries.definition: A regulatory behaviour in which cues that prompt a behaviour pattern are added, altered or removed.

entries.label: frequency of past behaviour
entries.definition: A behavioural frequency about the number of times a person has enacted a behaviour per unit of time.

entries.label: health-promoting behaviour
entries.definition: A health-related behaviour that improves the person’s health.

entries.label: monitoring behaviour
entries.definition: A knowledge development behaviour that involves gathering information over a period of time.

entries.label: spontaneous recovery
entries.definition: A <bodily process> in which a previously learned and then extinguished stimulus-response association becomes as least partially re-established without new pairing with a reinforcer.

entries.label: task complexity
entries.definition: A process attribute that is the complexity of thought or behaviour required to achieve a goal.

entries.label: extent of past behavioural achievement
entries.definition: A personal attribute that is the extent to which the person’s past behaviour has reached or exceeded a standard.

entries.label: leadership role
entries.definition: A social role in which the holder is given or creates authority to exert influence on other members of a social group.

entries.label: leadership role
entries.definition: A social role in which the holder is given or creates authority to exert influence on other members of a social group.

entries.label: rural area
entries.definition: A population and resource density that involves being outside of an urban or suburban area and has a low density of population and buildings.

entries.label: participant engagement with intervention
entries.definition: An individual human activity of an intervention participant within one or more parts of the intervention.
children: participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
participant engagement with behaviour change intervention
participant engagement with behaviour change intervention

entries.label: high frequency of past behaviour
entries.definition: A frequency of past behaviour that is above a threshold.

entries.label: high frequency of past behaviour
entries.definition: A frequency of past behaviour that is above a threshold.

entries.label: low frequency of past behaviour
entries.definition: A frequency of past behaviour that is below a threshold.

entries.label: monitoring of others
entries.definition: A <monitoring process> in which one observes others.

entries.label: monitoring process
entries.definition: A <mental process> in which one observes to inform their own behaviour.

entries.label: social norm monitoring
entries.definition: A <monitoring of others> to align one's behaviours with perceived social norms.

entries.label: awareness of standards
entries.definition: An <awareness> of a standard or benchmark.

entries.label: motivation to deviate
entries.definition: A <behavioural motivation> to act contrary to likely approval of others.

entries.label: social learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that involves a social influence process.

entries.label: social learning
entries.definition: <Learning> that involves a social influence process.

entries.label: willingness to cease behaviour
entries.definition: A <willingness to change behaviour> regarding the cessation of behaviours.

entries.label: willingness to change behaviour
entries.definition: A <bodily disposition> to change one's behaviour.

entries.label: degree of organisational hierarchy
entries.definition: An <organisational attribute> that is the strength and depth of formal authority relations between members of an organisation.

entries.label: degree of organisational hierarchy
entries.definition: An <organisational attribute> that is the strength and depth of formal authority relations between members of an organisation.

entries.label: organisational attribute
entries.definition: A <characteristic> that inheres in an organisation.

entries.label: response generalisation in learning
entries.definition: <Generalisation in learning> concerning the behavioural response.

entries.label: belief about accountability for the consequences of an action
entries.definition: A <belief about responsibility>  about whether one should be accountable for the outcomes of an event.

entries.label: generalisation in learning
entries.definition: <Associative learning> in which prior learning that an antecedent stimulus signals certain consequences of a behaviour spreads to a stimulus or behaviour that differs from the original stimulus or behaviour.

entries.label: hedonic goal
entries.definition: A <proximal goal> in which the end state is pleasure or relief from displeasure.

entries.label: subjective craving
entries.definition: A <subjective affective feeling> involving a strong desire or urge.

entries.label: drug withdrawal syndrome
entries.definition: A bodily disposition to undergo pathological processes characterized by physiological or psychological dysfunction that exists in an organism because of homeostatic disruption resulting from the cessation or reduction of repeated drug exposure.

entries.label: habituation
entries.definition: A decrease in a behavioral response to a repeated stimulus. This is exemplified by the failure of a person to show a startle response to a loud noise that has been repeatedly presented.

entries.label: sensitization
entries.definition: An increase in a behavioural response to a repeated stimulus.

entries.label: imitative learning
entries.definition: <Observational learning> in which new behaviours are acquired through imitation.

entries.label: cognitive process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that creates, modifies or has as participant some cognitive representation.

entries.label: arousal
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves heightened responding to an internal or external stimulus.

entries.label: arousal
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves heightened responding to an internal or external stimulus.

entries.label: arousal
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves heightened responding to an internal or external stimulus.

entries.label: thinking
entries.definition: A <cognitive process> that involves the manipulation of mental language and/or mental images.

entries.label: attending
entries.definition: A <mental process> whereby relevant aspects of one's mental experience are focused on specific targets.

entries.label: perception
entries.definition: A <mental process> which is a) produced by a causal process involving a part of the environment of the organism, and b) is experienced by the organism as being so caused, and c) in which the relevant part of the environment is thereby represented to the organism.

entries.label: perception
entries.definition: A <mental process> which is a) produced by a causal process involving a part of the environment of the organism, and b) is experienced by the organism as being so caused, and c) in which the relevant part of the environment is thereby represented to the organism.

entries.label: planning
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves mentally manipulating representations of steps in an imagined process which has some goal.

entries.label: cognitive representation
entries.definition: A <representation> which specifically depends on an anatomical structure in the cognitive system of an organism.

entries.label: belief
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to represent a proposition to be true.
children: belief about control over valued outcomes

entries.label: belief
entries.definition: A <mental disposition> to represent a proposition to be true.
children: belief about control over valued outcomes

entries.label: wanting
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves thinking about a state of affairs that is not yet the case together with a desire for that state of affairs to come about.

entries.label: subliminal process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that involves neuronal activity in response to a sensory stimulus but which is not the subject of consciousness.

entries.label: affective appraisal process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that gives rise to an appraisal.
children: accepting responsibility for changing one?s behaviour

entries.label: affective appraisal process
entries.definition: A <mental process> that gives rise to an appraisal.
children: accepting responsibility for changing one?s behaviour

entries.label: dose interval specification
entries.definition: An information content entity that specifies the minimum and maximum quantities of drug product or active ingredient in a dose administration.

entries.label: priming, psychological
entries.definition: Prior exposure to a stimulus facilitates or inhibits subsequent processing. Relevant to microdosing of informational interventions and subliminal activation.

entries.label: diffusion of innovation
entries.definition: The broad dissemination of new technologies, ideas, or practices through a population. Relevant to reach and population-level spread of behaviour change interventions.

entries.label: generalization, psychological
entries.definition: The tendency to apply a learned response to stimuli other than those in original learning. Operationalises Shepard Universal Law of Generalization for engagement transfer across contexts.

entries.label: nudging
entries.definition: Indirect suggestions and positive reinforcements influencing behaviour while maintaining freedom of choice. A form of choice architecture increasing engagement without restricting alternatives.

entries.label: dose-response relationship
entries.definition: The relationship between dose of an administered agent and the organism response. Applied to behaviour change: how effect size changes as a function of intervention dose.

entries.label: distributed practice
entries.definition: A learning strategy spreading practice over time with rest periods between sessions. Produces more durable effects than massed practice (spacing effect).

entries.label: massed practice
entries.definition: A learning strategy concentrating practice into a single session without rest. Faster initial learning but less durable retention than distributed practice.

entries.label: serial learning
entries.definition: Learning to respond to stimuli in a definite order. Relates to primacy and recency effects in schedule design.

entries.label: memory consolidation
entries.definition: Stabilization of newly acquired information in long-term memory following learning. Determines optimal inter-session intervals for intervention scheduling.

entries.label: arousal
entries.definition: Cortical alertness or readiness to perform resulting from sensory stimulation. Relates to Yerkes-Dodson law: optimal arousal determines optimal dose intensity.

entries.label: arousal
entries.definition: Cortical alertness or readiness to perform resulting from sensory stimulation. Relates to Yerkes-Dodson law: optimal arousal determines optimal dose intensity.

entries.label: discrimination, psychological
entries.definition: The ability to distinguish stimuli that differ in some dimension. Relates to Weber-Fechner law: just noticeable difference is a constant fraction of original stimulus.

entries.label: hormesis
entries.definition: A dose-response phenomenon characterised by low-dose stimulation and high-dose inhibition. Applied to behaviour change: low-intensity interventions may stimulate engagement while high-intensity may suppress it.

entries.label: decision making
entries.definition: Selective intellectual judgment when presented with complex alternatives. Includes prospect theory constructs: loss aversion, reference point, framing effects on engagement.

entries.label: psychotherapy, rational-emotive
entries.definition: Psychotherapy based on the ABC model: Activating event -> Belief -> Consequence. Reframed as MoA: belief appraisal drives behavioural engagement.

entries.label: implementation science
entries.definition: Scientific study of methods to promote uptake of evidence-based practices into routine use. Encompasses implementation intentions, fidelity, and drift constructs.

entries.label: implementation science
entries.definition: Scientific study of methods to promote uptake of evidence-based practices into routine use. Encompasses implementation intentions, fidelity, and drift constructs.

entries.label: circadian rhythm
entries.definition: A biological process recurring with approximately 24-hour regularity. Relevant to scheduling: time of day modulates receptivity to behavioural interventions.

entries.label: memory consolidation
entries.definition: Process by which newly acquired information is stabilised following learning. Required for durable behaviour change; determines optimal inter-session intervals.

entries.label: normalisation process theory
entries.definition: Theory explaining how new practices become embedded in routine work through coherence, cognitive participation, collective action, and reflexive monitoring. Relevant to implementation fidelity.

entries.label: re-aim framework
entries.definition: Framework evaluating public health impact across Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance. Formalises reach as a distinct evaluation dimension.

entries.label: re-aim framework
entries.definition: Framework evaluating public health impact across Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance. Formalises reach as a distinct evaluation dimension.

entries.label: implementation drift
entries.definition: Gradual deviation of an intervention from its original protocol over time. Describes the mechanics of how interventions transform during sustained delivery.

entries.label: implementation intentions
entries.definition: If-then plans specifying when, where, and how a behaviour will be performed. Enhance fidelity by translating abstract goals into concrete action sequences.

entries.label: implementation intentions
entries.definition: If-then plans specifying when, where, and how a behaviour will be performed. Enhance fidelity by translating abstract goals into concrete action sequences.

entries.label: flow, psychological
entries.definition: Complete absorption in a challenging activity where skill matches task demands. Represents optimal engagement; predicts sustained participation in behaviour change interventions.

entries.label: job demands-resources model
entries.definition: Job demands lead to burnout while resources foster engagement. Applied to behaviour change: intervention demands vs resources determine participant engagement vs dropout.

entries.label: sensemaking
entries.definition: The process by which people give meaning to collective experiences through their own frames. Determines how implementers interpret and adhere to protocols.

entries.label: behavioral momentum
entries.definition: The tendency for behaviour to persist in the face of disruption, analogous to physical momentum. Behaviour reinforced at high rates is more resistant to change; relevant to reinforcement scheduling.

entries.label: arrange satiation BCT
entries.definition: An <expose to stimulus BCT> that provides sustained exposure to a stimulus to reduce or extinguish an unwanted behaviour.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > associative learning BCT > alter external stimulus BCT > expose to stimulus BCT

entries.label: normative belief
entries.definition: A <perceived norm> regarding whether key others think one should perform a behaviour.
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > belief > perceived norm

entries.label: mass media mode of delivery
entries.definition: Informational mode of delivery of radio broadcast, television, online press and printed press to a mass audience.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > process profile > process attribute > BCI attribute > behaviour change intervention mode of delivery > informational mode of delivery

entries.label: social influence intervention through mass media
entries.definition: A behaviour change intervention that is an awareness of other people’s thoughts, feelings and actions BCT delivered through a mass media mode of delivery.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > intervention > behaviour change intervention

entries.label: provide positive consequence for behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <provide consequence for behaviour BCT> where the consequence is positive.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > behavioural consequence BCT > provide consequence for behaviour BCT
children: provide positive social consequence for behaviour BCT
provide positive consequence for incompatible behaviour BCT

entries.label: provide positive consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <provide consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT> where the consequence is positive.
entries.parent_label: provide consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > outcome consequence BCT > provide consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
children: provide reduced frequency of positive consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
provide positive social consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT

entries.label: remove aversive consequence for behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <remove consequence for behaviour BCT> where the consequence is aversive.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > behavioural consequence BCT > remove consequence for behaviour BCT
children: remove aversive social consequence for behaviour BCT
remove aversive material consequence for behaviour BCT

entries.label: remove aversive consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: A <remove consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT> in which the consequence is aversive.
entries.parent_label: remove consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > outcome consequence BCT > remove consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
children: remove aversive social consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT
remove aversive material consequence for outcome of behaviour BCT

entries.label: evaluation of information about behavioural self-efficacy
entries.definition: An <information evaluation process> about self-efficacy belief for a behaviour.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > bodily process > mental process > cognitive process > thinking > reflective thinking > information evaluation process

entries.label: negative reinforcement process
entries.definition: A <reinforcement process> in which the likelihood of behaviour is increased it being followed by termination or avoidance of a negatively appraised behaviour.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > reinforcement process

entries.label: positive reinforcement process
entries.definition: A <reinforcement process> in which the likelihood of a behaviour is increased by it being followed by a positively appraised event.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > reinforcement process

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour during routine activities
entries.definition: A <self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> that is enacted as part of activities that are routine.
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > belief > self-efficacy belief > self-efficacy belief for a behaviour

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour in the face of social pressure
entries.definition: A <situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> that is enacted against social pressire.
entries.parent_label: situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > belief > self-efficacy belief > self-efficacy belief for a behaviour > situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour

entries.label: self-efficacy belief for a behaviour under conditions of stress
entries.definition: A <situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour> that is enacted when under stress.
entries.parent_label: situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > belief > self-efficacy belief > self-efficacy belief for a behaviour > situational self-efficacy belief for a behaviour

entries.label: self-efficacy belief
entries.definition: A <belief> about one's capabilities to organise and execute a process or achieve an outcome.
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > belief
children: self-efficacy belief for avoiding a threat

entries.label: prompt intended action BCT
entries.definition: An <alter external stimulus BCT> that involves introducing an external stimulus to facilitate the behaviour for which an intention has previously been formed.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > associative learning BCT > alter external stimulus BCT

entries.label: cue BCT
entries.definition: An <alter external stimulus BCT> that introduces external information that is already associated with the behaviour in order to elicit that behaviour.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > associative learning BCT > alter external stimulus BCT

entries.label: context-specific non-enactment of behaviour BCT
entries.definition: An <advise specific behaviour BCT> that advises the person to refrain from engaging in the behaviour in a specified context.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > planned process > behaviour change technique > advise specific behaviour BCT

entries.label: individual human behaviour change
entries.definition: A process that results in a difference in enactment of some individual human behaviour or individual human behaviour pattern from what would have been the case otherwise.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process

entries.label: regulatory behaviour
entries.definition: An experience-related behaviour that involves monitoring and acting upon some parts of the person or their environment.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > bodily process > individual human behaviour > experience-related behaviour

entries.label: action planning
entries.definition: <Planning> that has as a part some goal that is specified in terms of some behaviour and a time or place where the behaviour is to be enacted.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > bodily process > mental process > planning

entries.label: learned stimulus-behaviour co-occurrence
entries.definition: A <learned stimulus-response co-occurrence> that involves a behavioural disposition in response to an internal or external event in the person's environment, which is acquired through habit learning.
entries.ancestor_path: continuant > specifically dependent continuant > realizable > disposition > bodily disposition > mental disposition > learned stimulus-reponse co-occurrence

entries.label: habitual behaviour
entries.definition: An <individual human behaviour> that results from a learnt stimulus-behaviour co-occurrence.
entries.ancestor_path: occurrent > process > bodily process > individual human behaviour
